target.title: fix(discord): preserve content-type and skip optimization for animated/WebP images
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Discord media uploads were sending files without a MIME content-type, causing some clients/bots to misidentify the file format. Additionally, WebP images were being converted to JPEG (losing quality) and animated images (GIF, WebP, APNG) were being converted/optimized, destroying animation frames.
- **Why it matters:** Users sending WebP images or animated GIFs/APNGs via Discord and the web provider received degraded or broken output — silent data-loss bugs.
- **What changed:** `toDiscordFileBlob` now accepts and forwards a `contentType` to the `Blob` constructor; `loadWebMediaInternal` skips image optimization for GIF, WebP, and any animated image (detected via sharp's `pages` metadata); a new `isAnimatedImage` helper was added to `image-ops.ts`.
- **What did NOT change:** Non-animated JPEG/PNG optimization paths are unchanged; no other channels are affected.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #41256
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- WebP images sent via Discord or the web provider are no longer silently converted to JPEG.
- Animated GIFs and animated WebP/APNG files are no longer passed through the image optimizer (which would strip frames).
- Discord uploads now include the correct MIME type in the `Blob`, so recipients see the right file format.
- Error messages for oversized GIF/WebP/animated images now include a more specific label (`GIF`, `WebP`, `Animated image`) instead of the generic `Media`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / Bun
- Model/provider: Any
- Integration/channel: Discord
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Send an animated GIF or a WebP image to a Discord channel via OpenClaw.
2. Observe the uploaded file in Discord.

### Expected

- Animated GIFs play in Discord.
- WebP images display as WebP (not JPEG).
- File MIME type is correctly reported.

### Actual (before fix)

- WebP images were converted to JPEG.
- Animated WebP images lost their animation frames.
- `Blob` had no `type`, so Discord received files with an unknown MIME type.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after — new tests added in `src/discord/send.shared.test.ts`, `src/media/image-ops.helpers.test.ts`, and `src/web/media.test.ts` covering all three fix areas.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: _fill in_
- Edge cases checked: static WebP (should still skip optimization), single-frame GIF
- What you did **not** verify: live Discord upload end-to-end (depends on bot credentials)

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert `src/web/media.ts` to re-enable optimization for WebP/animated; revert `src/discord/send.shared.ts` to drop content-type from `Blob`.
- Files/config to restore: `src/web/media.ts`, `src/discord/send.shared.ts`, `src/discord/send.components.ts`
- Known bad symptoms: WebP images silently downgraded to JPEG; animated images displayed as still frames; Discord upload MIME type shown as `application/octet-stream`.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Large WebP or animated images that previously would have been resized/optimized will now be passed through at full size and may hit the cap limit sooner.
  - Mitigation: The existing size-cap check still applies; users get a clear error (`WebP` / `Animated image` label) rather than silent conversion.

target.title: fix(qqbot): add exponential backoff, jitter, and circuit breaker to token refresh retry
target.body:
### Description

The token background refresh loop (`startBackgroundRefresh`) currently retries with a fixed 5-second delay on every failure. When `bots.qq.com` returns a transient 503 (STGW gateway), this creates a tight retry storm.

While the cross-plugin network isolation was already fixed in v2026.6.8 (PR #89015 made token fetch use per-request dispatchers via `fetchWithSsrFGuard`), this PR addresses the remaining QQBot self-preservation concern:

- **API politeness**: hammering Tencent's API every 5s during a sustained outage is bad practice and may trigger rate limiting
- **Self-recovery**: exponential backoff lets the service recover naturally when the endpoint comes back
- **Log hygiene**: fewer error log entries during transient failures

### Changes

**`extensions/qqbot/src/engine/api/token.ts`**:

1. `BackgroundRefreshOptions` interface — added 3 optional fields (backward-compatible):
   - `maxRetryDelayMs` (default: 120s)
   - `circuitBreakerThreshold` (default: 6)
   - `circuitBreakerCooldownMs` (default: 5min)

2. New exported pure function `resolveRetryDelayMs()` — calculates retry delay with:
   - **Exponential backoff**: `baseDelayMs * 2^(retry-1)`, capped at `maxRetryDelayMs`
   - **Jitter**: ±30% (random 70%-130%) to avoid thundering herd
   - **Circuit breaker**: after threshold, pauses for cooldown (no jitter, exact value)

3. `startBackgroundRefresh` loop body — tracks `consecutiveRetries` counter:
   - Reset to 0 on every successful token refresh
   - On failure, uses `resolveRetryDelayMs()` instead of hardcoded 5s

**`extensions/qqbot/src/engine/api/token.test.ts`** — 3 new test cases for `resolveRetryDelayMs`, each running 50 iterations to verify jitter bounds.

### Retry timing

| Consecutive failures | Delay (approx) | Cumulative |
|---------------------|----------------|------------|
| 1 | ~5s | ~5s |
| 2 | ~10s | ~15s |
| 3 | ~20s | ~35s |
| 4 | ~40s | ~75s |
| 5 | ~80s | ~2.5min |
| 6 | ~120s (capped) | ~4.5min |
| 7+ | 5min (circuit breaker) | — |

### Backward compatibility

All new fields are optional with sensible defaults. The single call site (`gateway-connection.ts:209`) passes `{ log }` only, so it will use all defaults without changes.

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches

target.title: fix(gateway): forward child session node events to spawnedBy subscribers
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: gateway node subscriptions are keyed by exact `sessionKey`, so child-session node events disappear when only the parent session is subscribed.
- Why it matters: spawned agent chat activity can look dead from the gateway/node subscriber side even while work is actually running.
- What changed: added a direct-miss fallback in `createNodeSubscriptionManager.sendToSession()` that walks `spawnedBy` lineage, canonicalizes ancestor keys, and routes to the first ancestor with subscribers; added a focused regression test and changelog entry.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not change ACP execution, session transcript bridging, or broader subagent progress/status surfaces.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #34852
- Related #34905

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Spawned child-session node events now reach parent-session node subscribers when only the parent session is subscribed.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local dev checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): gateway node subscriptions
- Relevant config (redacted): default test harness

### Steps

1. Subscribe a node to a parent session key.
2. Emit a node event under a spawned child session key whose `spawnedBy` points to that parent.
3. Observe delivery through the node subscription manager.

### Expected

- Parent subscriber receives the child-session event.

### Actual

- Before this fix, delivery stopped on the child key miss and no event was sent.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: child-session events fall back through `spawnedBy` lineage to a subscribed parent; canonicalized parent lookup works; repeated sends reuse the cached ancestor lookup.
- Edge cases checked: no direct subscriber regression; partial object assertion accounts for `payloadJSON`; focused gateway test suite passes locally.
- What you did **not** verify: full gateway end-to-end node streaming against a live spawned-agent session.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR to restore exact-key-only subscription routing.
- Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/server-node-subscriptions.ts`, `src/gateway/gateway-misc.test.ts`, `CHANGELOG.md`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: node subscribers receiving unexpected ancestor-routed events beyond the intended `spawnedBy` chain.

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: lineage fallback could repeatedly touch session storage on hot miss paths.
  - Mitigation: fallback only runs on direct misses, uses a bounded depth, and caches the resolved ancestor subscriber key.

Supersedes the dirty branch state in #34905.

target.title: Security: harden gateway timeouts and auth store sealing
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `GatewayClient.request()` could hang indefinitely, auth stores were always plaintext at rest, and mirrored transcript writes did not re-assert restrictive permissions after append.
- Why it matters: hung gateway RPCs leak pending state and stall callers; optional at-rest sealing gives operators a low-friction hardening path for OpenClaw-owned auth JSON stores.
- What changed: added bounded gateway request timeouts, added optional `OPENCLAW_PASSPHRASE` sealing for `auth-profiles.json` and legacy `oauth.json`, added regression coverage, and re-applied `0600` perms after mirrored transcript writes.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not implement full session transcript encryption because `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent` and multiple local readers still depend on raw JSONL session files.
- Thanks @alamine42 for the original hardening report, and @vincentkoc for review context.

- When `OPENCLAW_PASSPHRASE` is set, OpenClaw-owned auth stores are written in sealed form and require that same env var to be read.
- Mirrored transcript writes now best-effort reset the transcript file mode to `0600` after append.

## Security Impact (required)

2. Run repo-level `pnpm check`.
3. Run repo-level `pnpm build`.

### Actual

target.title: AI-assisted: Gateway hook event routing respects target agent session
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Hook completion and error system events were always enqueued to the default agent main session, even when the hook ran against another agent.
- Why it matters: In multi-agent setups this leaked hook summaries, including email-derived content, across agent boundaries.
- What changed: Hook system events now resolve the effective target agent and enqueue to that agent's main session; tests now assert `hooks`-targeted events stay out of the default session.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Hook execution, delivery behavior, and wake-hook routing were not otherwise changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- Related #24016

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Hook completion/error summaries now appear only in the target agent's main session instead of leaking into the default agent session.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes`)

  This reduces data exposure by scoping hook-generated system events to the intended agent session. No broader data access was added.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Hook gateway endpoints
- Relevant config (redacted): multi-agent config with default `main` agent and `hooks` agent

### Steps

1. Configure hooks with a target `agentId` of `hooks`.
2. Trigger `/hooks/agent` with a non-delivered hook run.
3. Inspect queued system events for `agent:main:main` and `agent:hooks:main`.

### Expected

- The hook summary is queued only in the target agent's main session.

### Actual

- Before this change it was queued in the default agent's main session.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets

## Human Verification (required)

target.title: Gateway: allow extension origins in browser allowlist
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` could not explicitly allowlist `chrome-extension://` because the origin checker normalized extension origins through `URL.origin`, which becomes `"null"` for that scheme. - Why it matters: extension sidepanel clients had to rely on `["*"]` to get through browser-origin WS checks, which weakened the intended hardening. - What changed: the origin matcher now keeps exact URL parsing/validation but falls back to exact `scheme://host` matching for non-standard origins, so `chrome-extension://` can be allowlisted exactly; focused unit and handshake tests were added. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): no wildcard/prefix expansion, no acceptance of literal `Origin: null`, no changes to token/device auth, host-header fallback, or non-browser client auth behavior. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #35035 (issue mentions app://* origin, but it's the same underlying problem) - Related #35396, #35057 (both PRS were fixes for this problem but were closed because the authors were banned AFAIK) ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` can now contain exact extension origins such as `chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop`, and those browser-origin Gateway WS connects will succeed when otherwise authenticated. - Operators no longer need `["*"]` solely to let a Chrome extension sidepanel client connect. - Existing exact matches for normal `http(s)` origins, wildcard behavior, host-header fallback, and literal `Origin: null` rejection remain unchanged. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` (strictly speaking this allows chrome extensions/electron+tauri apps to connect but I think calling it a new capability is a stretch) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: `N/A` ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: local Bun + Vitest - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): Gateway WS browser-origin client; Chrome extension origin emulated in handshake tests via `ws` - Relevant config (redacted): `gateway.auth.mode="token"`, `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins=["chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop"]` ### Steps 1. Configure the Gateway with token auth and `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins=["chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop"]`. 2. Open a WS connection that sends `Origin: chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop` and a valid `connect` request. 3. Observe the handshake result. ### Expected - An exactly allowlisted extension origin should be accepted, just like an exactly allowlisted `https://...` origin. ### Actual - Before this fix, the request was rejected with `origin not allowed` because the checker collapsed the extension origin to `"null"` internally. - After this fix, the handshake succeeds and returns `hello-ok`. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: whitelisted the chrome extension I'm developing and successfully connected to the Gateway via WS - Edge cases checked: N/A - What you did **not** verify: did not try with other protocols (`app://`, `tauri://`) ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: `N/A` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this commit; if extension access is urgently blocked, temporarily fall back to `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins=["*"]` until the regression is understood. - Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/origin-check.ts`; optionally restore prior `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` values in config. - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: an exactly allowlisted `chrome-extension://` still being rejected; malformed/non-matching non-standard origins unexpectedly being accepted; regressions in existing `http(s)` origin allowlisting.

target.title: feat(hooks): add agent and tool lifecycle boundaries
target.body:
## Why Agent execution lacked complete lifecycle boundaries around thinking, response generation, and tool execution, reducing traceability for observability and policy systems. This PR adds those boundaries and ensures tool lifecycle hooks carry `toolCallId` context when available. lobster-biscuit ## Split Context This PR was accidentally closed after a messed up rebase https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/12583 which was originally split from closed umbrella PR #9761: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/9761. ## Detailed Changes - Added internal agent lifecycle hook emissions: - `agent:thinking:start` / `agent:thinking:end` - `agent:response:start` / `agent:response:end` - `agent:tool:start` / `agent:tool:end` - Added `runAfterToolCallHook` wiring for tool execution lifecycle completion - Propagated `toolCallId` through before/after tool hook contexts where available - Extended client tool definition adapter to pass `toolCallId` and run after-tool hook flow - Added focused tests for agent lifecycle emissions and tool lifecycle behavior ## Related Links, Issues and Resolution - Closed source PR: #12583 AND #9761 - Related issues: - Closes #7724 - Closes #7597 - Closes #5513 - #7985 - #6095 - #6264 - #6885

Greptile Summary

This PR adds complete lifecycle boundaries for agent execution (thinking, response generation, and tool execution), improving observability and traceability for policy systems. The implementation propagates `toolCallId` through tool lifecycle hooks and ensures consistent hook firing across different execution paths. Key improvements: - Split lifecycle hook logic into dedicated `lifecycle-hooks.ts` module - Ensured `agent:thinking:start/end` fire for all agent runs - Added conditional `agent:response:start/end` hooks that only fire when response output is generated - Propagated `toolCallId` to `before_tool_call` and `after_tool_call` hooks - Added `runAfterToolCallHook` wiring for both success and error paths in tool execution - Comprehensive test coverage validates hook emissions in different scenarios

Confidence Score: 5/5

- Safe to merge - well-structured refactoring with comprehensive test coverage - The changes are well-architected with proper error handling throughout. The new `lifecycle-hooks.ts` module cleanly separates concerns, all hook emission paths include error handling that logs warnings without failing execution, and the test coverage validates the core lifecycle hook behavior. The `toolCallId` propagation is handled consistently with proper fallbacks. - No files require special attention Last reviewed commit: d48824c

commands.author: snowzlmbot

target.title: fix(context-engine): avoid quarantining read-only discovery factories
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #96335.

OpenClaw can load plugin context-engine registrations while doing read-only plugin discovery. Some plugins, including lossless-claw 0.13.1, intentionally capture that read-only lifecycle in the registered factory and reject stateful engine initialization from that phase. Current main stores that discovery factory like a runtime factory, then `resolveContextEngine()` invokes it, quarantines the selected plugin engine, and falls back to `legacy` for the process.

This PR keeps read-only discovery useful for metadata collection, but prevents discovery-only context-engine factories from being constructed or quarantined as runtime failures.

## Summary

- Adds a context-engine registration lifecycle marker:
  - `runtime`
  - `readOnlyDiscovery`
- Marks plugin API `registerContextEngine()` calls as `runtime` only during full plugin activation.
- Marks discovery/tool-discovery plugin registrations as `readOnlyDiscovery`.
- Prevents read-only discovery registrations from replacing an already-active runtime registration from the same plugin owner.
- Makes `resolveContextEngine()` fall back to the default engine without quarantine when the selected engine is only registered from read-only discovery.
- Keeps runtime factory failures quarantined exactly as before.
- Keeps runtime plugin activation able to replace a prior read-only discovery registration and activate the configured engine.

## Scope Boundary

This intentionally does **not** change the `lightContext` cron contract from #96334. That issue is currently labeled by ClawSweeper as `clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr`, `clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review`, and `clawsweeper:needs-product-decision`, so this PR avoids changing that separate product behavior.

## Regression Coverage

Added focused coverage for the lossless-claw-style lifecycle sequence:

1. A plugin-owned context engine is registered from read-only discovery with a factory that throws the reported error: `Engine initialization is disabled during read-only plugin registration`.
2. `resolveContextEngine()` does not invoke that factory.
3. The selected engine is not quarantined.
4. Runtime/full activation replaces the discovery registration and resolves the plugin engine.
5. A later read-only discovery pass does not overwrite the runtime-safe factory.

## Evidence

Local lightweight preflight:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/context-engine/context-engine.test.ts` — passed, 46 tests.
- `corepack pnpm format src/context-engine/registry.ts src/context-engine/context-engine.test.ts src/plugins/registry.ts` — applied formatting to changed files.
- `corepack pnpm format:check src/context-engine/registry.ts src/context-engine/context-engine.test.ts src/plugins/registry.ts` — passed.
- `git diff --check` — passed.

Live / reproducibility notes:

- GitHub Actions for this PR ran on the exact submitted head `0d7cf30c32d2cde431738ba3f46e3ae9640d8902`.
- Official Real behavior proof gate passed: <https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28089066083/job/83162223916>.
- Configured plugin context-engine proof passed on the exact PR head: <https://github.com/snowzlmbot/openclaw/actions/runs/28093889925>.
  - Artifact: `context-engine-readonly-registration-proof-0d7cf30c32d2cde431738ba3f46e3ae9640d8902`.
  - The proof dynamically creates a real OpenClaw plugin with `kind: ["context-engine"]`, a real `openclaw.plugin.json`, and a runtime file loaded through `loadOpenClawPlugins()`.
  - It configures `plugins.slots.contextEngine = "proof-context-engine"`, then runs a read-only discovery load followed by a full runtime load.
  - Discovery result: `resolvedEngineId=legacy`, `warningCount=1`, `errorCount=0`, and the warning explicitly says the engine is registered for read-only discovery only and falls back without quarantine.
  - Runtime result: `resolvedEngineId=proof-context-engine`; plugin status is `loaded`; `contextEngineIds` contains `proof-context-engine`.
  - Assertions passed: exact head recorded, discovery fallback without quarantine, read-only factory not invoked/quarantined, runtime activation resolves the configured plugin engine, and the runtime registry records the context-engine registration.
- The regression test uses the real OpenClaw context-engine registry/resolve path and reproduces the exact reported read-only initialization error string.
- The public npm registry does not expose `lossless-claw@0.13.1` as `lossless-claw`, so the test cannot install that package directly from npm. The configured-plugin proof reproduces the observed lifecycle failure through OpenClaw's public plugin-owned context-engine registration path.
- A local filtered `src/plugins/loader.test.ts -t "context engine"` run was stopped after it produced no timely output, to avoid turning local preflight into a broad/heavy test run. Broader verification is delegated to GitHub Actions.

## Risk

Low-to-moderate. The change is intentionally narrow around context-engine factory lifecycle handling. The main behavior change is that discovery-only registrations no longer poison runtime resolution through quarantine. If a configured engine never reaches full activation, resolution still falls back to `legacy`, but now it does so without incorrectly quarantining the plugin for the process.

Release notes

Sourced from gradle-wrapper's releases.

9.6.0

The Gradle team is excited to announce Gradle 9.6.0.

Here are the highlights of this release:

  • Improved Configuration Cache hit rates
  • Additional CLI rendering options
  • Important project hierarchy lookup deprecations

Read the Release Notes

We would like to thank the following community members for their contributions to this release of Gradle: Aharnish Solanki, Benedikt Johannes, Devendra Reddy Pennabadi, Dmytro Rodionov, Dreeam, Elías Hernández Rodríguez, Eng Zer Jun, FinlayRJW, Kamal Kansal, Marcono1234, Nelson Osacky, Philip Wedemann, Ravi, Roberto Perez Alcolea, Ryan Schmitt, Sebastian Schuberth, seunghun.ham, sk-reddy17, Suvrat Acharya, Vedant Madane.

Upgrade instructions

Switch your build to use Gradle 9.6.0 by updating your wrapper:

./gradlew :wrapper --gradle-version=9.6.0 && ./gradlew :wrapper

See the Gradle 9.x upgrade guide to learn about deprecations, breaking changes and other considerations when upgrading.

For Java, Groovy, Kotlin and Android compatibility, see the full compatibility notes.

Reporting problems

If you find a problem with this release, please file a bug on GitHub Issues adhering to our issue guidelines. If you're not sure you're encountering a bug, please use the forum.

We hope you will build happiness with Gradle, and we look forward to your feedback via Twitter or on GitHub.

9.6.0 RC3

Updates `androidx.compose:compose-bom` from 2026.05.01 to 2026.06.00 Updates `androidx.core:core-ktx` from 1.18.0 to 1.19.0 Updates `androidx.lifecycle:lifecycle-runtime-ktx` from 2.10.0 to 2.11.0 Updates `org.commonmark:commonmark` from 0.28.0 to 0.29.0
Release notes

Sourced from org.commonmark:commonmark's releases.

commonmark-java 0.29.0

Added

  • Support rendering GFM task list items to Markdown (#433)
  • Support rendering YAML front matter to Markdown (#434)
  • Alerts
    • Allow customizing HTML attributes for alert title <p> tag via AttributeProvider (#427)
    • New configuration for AlertsExtension to allow authors to provide custom titles per alert. See the custom titles section of the alerts README for more information. (#430)
    • New configuration for AlertsExtension to allow alerts to be nested within other blocks (including other alerts). See this section of the alerts README for more information. (#430)
    • New configuration for AlertsExtension to allow the set of alert types (including standard GFM types) to be completely overwritten. (#435) 
      var extension = AlertsExtension.builder()
        .setAllowedTypes(Map.ofEntries(
                Map.entry("IMPORTANT", "Important"),
                Map.entry("WARNING", "Warning")
                Map.entry("BUG", "Known Bug")
        ))
        .build();
    • Support rendering alerts to text (#437)

target.title: [AI] fix(daemon): set default NODE_OPTIONS --max-old-space-size in gateway service env
target.body:
## Summary

Managed gateway and node services now receive a default `--max-old-space-size=8192` heap flag via generated systemd/launchd/schtasks service files, with an optional `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_HEAP_MB` env override. Ambient `NODE_OPTIONS` from the host process are never persisted into managed service environments.

- Problem: Gateway crash-loops with default Node heap (~4 GB) under sustained load. `openclaw daemon install` did not set any heap configuration in generated service files.
- Solution: Generate only the OpenClaw-owned `--max-old-space-size` flag in managed service environments. Support `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_HEAP_MB` override. Explicitly drop all ambient `NODE_OPTIONS` from the host process.
- What changed: `src/daemon/service-env.ts` — `resolveServiceNodeOptions()` generates only the validated heap flag; `buildCommonServiceEnvironment()` applies it as `NODE_OPTIONS`
- What did NOT change: Proxy env, CA certs, PATH, HOME, and all other service environment fields; `daemon install` workflow

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Fixes #96203
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

Gateway crash-loops with default Node heap (~4 GB) under sustained load. The `openclaw daemon install` path did not configure any heap limit for managed services. This PR adds a default `--max-old-space-size=8192` (8 GB) for gateway and node daemon services, configurable via `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_HEAP_MB`.

### Security: Ambient NODE_OPTIONS dropped

The original implementation read `env.NODE_OPTIONS` from the host process and preserved or appended to it. This could persist hostile flags (`--require`, `--inspect`, `--expose-gc`) into managed services, re-opening a startup preload boundary. The fix generates only the OpenClaw-owned `--max-old-space-size` flag and never reads host `NODE_OPTIONS`.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed**: Managed gateway/node service environments receive only the OpenClaw-owned `--max-old-space-size` heap flag. Generated systemd unit files contain `Environment=NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192`. Hostile ambient flags are dropped.

- **Real environment tested**: Linux x86_64, Node v24.13.1, commit `3ab0222`, OpenClaw v2026.6.9

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Full test suite (97 tests)
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/service-env.test.ts --run

# Generated systemd unit proof (no daemon install needed)
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e '
import { buildServiceEnvironment, buildNodeServiceEnvironment } from "./src/daemon/service-env.ts";
import { buildSystemdUnit } from "./src/daemon/systemd-unit.ts";
// Gateway default, heap override, node service, hostile NODE_OPTIONS, reinstall
'
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/service-env.test.ts --run

 RUN  v4.1.8

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  97 passed (97)
   Duration  1.19s
```

Generated systemd unit file proof (equivalent to `openclaw daemon install` output):

```console
=== 1. Generated systemd gateway unit (default) ===
  Description=OpenClaw Gateway
  ExecStart=/usr/bin/node --import tsx /opt/openclaw/src/gateway/server.ts
  Environment=NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192
  Environment=OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MARKER=openclaw
  Environment=OPENCLAW_SERVICE_KIND=gateway
  Environment=OPENCLAW_SERVICE_VERSION=2026.6.9

=== 2. Generated systemd gateway unit (with OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_HEAP_MB=6144) ===
  Environment=NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=6144

=== 3. Generated systemd node unit (default) ===
  Environment=NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192
  Environment=OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MARKER=openclaw
  Environment=OPENCLAW_SERVICE_KIND=node
  Environment=OPENCLAW_SERVICE_VERSION=2026.6.9

=== 4. Ambient NODE_OPTIONS security proof ===
Input NODE_OPTIONS:  --require /tmp/evil.js --inspect=0.0.0.0:9229
Output NODE_OPTIONS: --max-old-space-size=8192
--require dropped:  true
--inspect dropped:  true

=== 5. Reinstall/upgrade scenario ===
NODE_OPTIONS after reinstall: --max-old-space-size=8192
```

Behavior matrix:

| Scenario | Generated `NODE_OPTIONS` | Hostile flags |
|----------|--------------------------|:--:|
| Default gateway install | `--max-old-space-size=8192` | — |
| `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_HEAP_MB=6144` | `--max-old-space-size=6144` | — |
| `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_HEAP_MB=0` | `undefined` (disabled) | — |
| `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_HEAP_MB=invalid` | `--max-old-space-size=8192` (fallback) | — |
| Node service install | `--max-old-space-size=8192` | — |
| Host with `--require evil.js` | `--max-old-space-size=8192` | ✅ dropped |
| Host with `--inspect=0.0.0.0:9229` | `--max-old-space-size=8192` | ✅ dropped |
| Reinstall/upgrade | `--max-old-space-size=8192` | ✅ clean |

- **Observed result after fix**:
  1. Generated systemd unit files contain `Environment=NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192`
  2. `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_HEAP_MB` correctly overrides the heap size in generated service files
  3. `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_HEAP_MB=0` correctly disables the heap flag
  4. Hostile ambient flags (`--require`, `--inspect`) are completely dropped
  5. Both gateway and node services receive the heap configuration
  6. Reinstall/upgrade regenerates clean NODE_OPTIONS
  7. All 97 tests pass

- **What was not tested**: Actual `openclaw daemon install` with `systemctl` on a real Linux host (requires root). The generated systemd unit content is identical — `daemon install` calls `buildSystemdUnit(buildServiceEnvironment(...))` internally.

## Root Cause

The original `resolveServiceNodeOptions()` read `env.NODE_OPTIONS` from the host process and appended `--max-old-space-size` to preserve existing flags. This was intended to be non-destructive but created a security boundary violation: any flag in the host `NODE_OPTIONS` would be persisted into managed services.

## Regression Test Plan

- Target: `src/daemon/service-env.test.ts`
- Covered scenarios: 97 tests total, including:
  - Default `--max-old-space-size=8192` when no NODE_OPTIONS present
  - Ambient NODE_OPTIONS with heap flag → dropped, default used
  - Ambient NODE_OPTIONS with other flags → dropped, default used
  - `--require` hostile flag → dropped
  - `--inspect` hostile flag → dropped
  - `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_HEAP_MB` override
  - `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_HEAP_MB` invalid → fallback to default
  - `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_HEAP_MB=0` → disabled
  - `buildNodeServiceEnvironment` includes NODE_OPTIONS

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users who set `NODE_OPTIONS` in their shell environment will no longer see those flags propagated to managed services. Only the `--max-old-space-size` heap flag is set. Users who need additional Node flags must use `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_HEAP_MB` or wait for an explicit allowlist mechanism.

## Security Impact (required)

- [x] New permissions/capabilities? No
- [x] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [x] New/changed network calls? No
- [x] Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- [x] Data access scope changed? No
- [x] **Security hardening**: Hostile `NODE_OPTIONS` (`--require`, `--inspect`) from the host process are explicitly dropped from managed service environments

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Generated systemd gateway unit, heap override, node service unit, hostile flag injection, reinstall/upgrade
- Edge cases checked: Invalid `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_HEAP_MB`, zero-disable, multi-flag ambient NODE_OPTIONS
- What you did NOT verify: `systemctl daemon-reload && systemctl start` on a real Linux host

## Best-fix Verdict

Only the OpenClaw-owned `--max-old-space-size` flag is generated. Ambient `NODE_OPTIONS` are never read. This is the narrowest fix that addresses the OOM issue without re-opening the startup preload boundary.

- **Best fix**: Yes
- **Refactor needed**: No
- **Alternative considered**: Preserving selected benign NODE_OPTIONS — rejected because there is no documented allowlist and maintainers should decide which flags are safe

## AI Assistance 🤖 (required for AI-assisted PRs)

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **AI model**: deepseek-v4-pro
- **Human confirmed understanding of code changes**: Yes

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Highest-risk area**: Low-memory hosts (≤8 GB) where the 8192 MB default could cause swap pressure. The `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_HEAP_MB` override allows operators to tune this.
- **Mitigation**: Operators on low-memory hosts should set `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_HEAP_MB` to a lower value (e.g. 4096). The default was chosen to match the documented gateway memory requirements.
- **Compatibility impact**: Services that previously received ambient `NODE_OPTIONS` will now only receive `--max-old-space-size`. This is a security improvement.

---

Fixes #96203

target.title: fix: classify provider code-only failover errors
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
Structured assistant errors that only carry an error `code` (no HTTP status, no body message) were falling through to `null` in the failover reason classifier, preventing model failover from working. Provider-native codes like OpenAI `SERVER_ERROR`, Google `DEADLINE_EXCEEDED`, and Anthropic `API_ERROR` were not recognized.

## Why This Change Was Made
Provider-specific code-to-reason mappings now live in each provider's `classifyFailoverReason` hook (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google-family), keeping the core `classifyFailoverReasonFromCode` generic. This prevents provider policy from becoming shared core policy that drifts as providers evolve.

## User Impact
Code-only provider errors correctly trigger model failover. When the primary provider returns `SERVER_ERROR` (OpenAI), `DEADLINE_EXCEEDED` (Google), or `API_ERROR` (Anthropic), the failover classifier recognizes them and attempts a fallback model.

## Evidence

**Tests:**
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/failover-error.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1) / Tests  91 passed (91)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/errors-provider-structured-signals.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/provider-error-patterns.test.ts --run
 Test Files  2 passed (2) / Tests  44 passed (44)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1) / Tests  61 passed (61)
```

target.title: fix(telegram): prevent RICH_MESSAGE_EMAIL_INVALID by stripping mailto links and adding rich-message fallback
target.body: ## What Problem This Solves Fixes an issue where Telegram `/status` slash command fails to deliver its reply when `richMessages=true` is configured. The Gateway executes the slash command, but Telegram's `sendRichMessage` API rejects the payload with `RICH_MESSAGE_EMAIL_INVALID`, leaving the user with no response. Closes #96363 ## Why This Change Was Made Two gaps caused the #96363 failure: 1. **`isTelegramRichLinkHref` accepted `mailto:` hrefs.** When the `/status` output contains email-like text (e.g. auth profile labels like `oauth (default (user@example.com))`), markdown-it's linkify plugin auto-detects the email and creates an `` anchor. Telegram's `sendRichMessage` API then validates the email entity and rejects the entire message with `RICH_MESSAGE_EMAIL_INVALID` when the address is malformed or unsupported. 2. **No fallback when `sendRichMessage` rejects with `RICH_MESSAGE_*_INVALID`.** When the rich-message send fails, the entire delivery is dropped silently. The user sees no `/status` response at all. ## Changes ### Layer 1 — Prevention (`format.ts`) - Removed `mailto:` from `isTelegramRichLinkHref` regex. Now `buildTelegramLink` returns `null` for mailto: hrefs, causing the link to render as plain visible text instead of an `` tag. This mirrors the existing behavior for local/relative file paths that cause `RICH_MESSAGE_URL_INVALID`. ### Layer 2 — Recovery (`send.ts`) - Added `isTelegramRichMessageInvalidError` to detect `RICH_MESSAGE_*_INVALID` error patterns (covers `EMAIL_INVALID`, `URL_INVALID`, and any future `_INVALID` variants). - Modified `sendChunkedText` to catch `RICH_MESSAGE_*_INVALID` errors from `sendTelegramRichTextChunks` and fall back to plain-text delivery via `sendMessage`. This ensures the user always receives the content, even if a rare rich-message entity validation failure occurs. ### Test coverage - Updated existing `mailto:` test to reflect the new behavior (label text preserved, `` tag dropped). - Added test: auto-detected emails remain as plain text in rich HTML. - Added test: `RICH_MESSAGE_EMAIL_INVALID` triggers plain-text fallback. - Added test: `RICH_MESSAGE_URL_INVALID` triggers plain-text fallback. ## User Impact - Users with `richMessages=true` who have email addresses in auth profile labels will now receive `/status` replies successfully instead of seeing no response. - Email addresses remain visible as plain text in rich messages; they are just no longer clickable. This is an acceptable trade-off vs. the entire message being dropped. - HTTPS, `tg://`, `tel:`, and `#fragment` links continue to work as clickable rich links. - If any `RICH_MESSAGE_*_INVALID` error still occurs despite the prevention layer, the system now falls back to plain-text delivery instead of silently dropping the message. ## Evidence **Real behavior proof (source-level verification):** ``` Test 1: Auto-detected email in status output Input: 🔑 oauth (default (user@example.com)) HTML: 🔑 oauth (default (user@example.com)) Has mailto: false ✓ (mailto link should NOT be generated) Test 2: Explicit mailto: link Input: [support](mailto:help@company.com) HTML: support Has mailto: false ✓ (mailto link should be stripped, label preserved) Test 3: Normal HTTPS link Input: [docs](https://example.com/docs) HTML: docs Has https href: true ✓ (HTTPS links should still work) Test 4: Full /status output Has mailto: false ✓ (no mailto links should be generated) Has user@example.com text: true ✓ (email text should still be visible) ``` Command: `node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "..."` (inline verification of `markdownToTelegramRichHtml`) **Test suite results:** ``` ✓ |extension-telegram| extensions/telegram/src/format.test.ts (59 tests) — 59 passed ✓ |extension-telegram| extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts (136 tests) — 136 passed ``` Command: `pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/format.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts` **What was not tested:** Live Telegram Bot API `sendRichMessage` call against a real Telegram bot with `richMessages=true`. The fix is validated through source-level unit tests and HTML output verification. **Proof limitations:** The `RICH_MESSAGE_*_INVALID` fallback path is exercised by mocking the `sendRichMessage` rejection; no live Telegram API integration test was performed.

target.title: fix: allow trusted host-read html after outbound staging
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes Telegram/file outbound delivery for trusted generated `text/html` reports after the attachment is staged into the OpenClaw outbound media store.

PR #87982 allowed generated HTML reports under the OpenClaw temp root, but the live outbound flow loads the original file, copies it to `~/.openclaw/media/outbound/<uuid>.html`, then the channel adapter loads that staged copy again. The second load no longer lives under the temp root, so host-read rejected it and the Telegram message was silently dropped.

This extends the trusted-host-read HTML boundary to the outbound-staged copy through a **content-bound provenance marker** persisted in the shared SQLite state DB, instead of trusting the staging directory by path alone. Trust is bound to the exact staged bytes and verified read-once at host-read time.

Fixes #90557.

## Design

When `resolveOutboundAttachmentFromUrl` stages a file whose source was a trusted-generated HTML path (under the temp root), it records a provenance row in a new `outbound_media_provenance` table keyed by the staged file's realpath, storing the `sha256` + `size_bytes` of the staged bytes (`kind = "trusted-generated-html"`, `version = 1`).

At host-read time, a file inside `<mediaDir>/outbound/` is trusted **only if** a provenance row exists for its realpath **and** the row's `sha256` + size match the bytes that `loadWebMedia` already read. Files under the temp root keep their existing unconditional trust. The provenance row is pruned in lockstep with the staged file by `pruneStaleTrustedGeneratedHtmlMarkers`, wired into the media-store retention pass (`cleanOldMedia`).

Preserved guards (unchanged): regular file, not a symlink, `nlink === 1`, final read still validates text shape and HTML document shape.

## ClawSweeper P1 resolution

Two prior P1 code findings are addressed in this revision (independently confirmed sound by a second-model review, which also flagged the read-once requirement below):

- **Path-keyed provenance bypass** → **content-bound + read-once.** The marker now stores `sha256` + `size_bytes`, and host-read trust is decided by hashing the **same buffer `loadWebMedia` already read** (no second filesystem read, so there is no hash-then-read TOCTOU window). A file whose bytes are replaced after the marker is written is rejected. New regression test: stage bytes `B1` + mark with the real digest, overwrite the same path with different bytes `B2`, assert host-read is rejected with `path-not-allowed`.
- **Swallowed marker write failure** → **propagate.** `resolveOutboundAttachmentFromUrl` no longer swallows `markTrustedGeneratedHtmlPath` errors; on failure it best-effort unlinks the staged file and rethrows, so an infrastructure/write error surfaces through the normal media-failure path instead of deferring to a later silent Telegram reload drop. New test asserts the rethrow and the best-effort `rm`.

The trust expansion is therefore provenance-gated, content-bound, fail-closed, and persisted in SQLite (matching the canonical-storage policy; an in-memory allowlist would break delayed/restarted delivery).

## Test plan

```text
pnpm db:kysely:gen && pnpm db:kysely:check          # generated types in sync with the new table
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/media/web-media.test.ts src/media/outbound-attachment.test.ts
```

## Real behavior proof

### Deterministic real-code-path before/after (2026-06-24)

This exercises the **same production helpers the Telegram channel adapter uses** — `resolveOutboundAttachmentFromUrl` (stage) followed by `loadWebMedia(stagedPath, { hostReadCapability: true })` (the second reload) — on two worktrees that differ by **only this PR's diff**:

- **baseline** = `301213a05f` (this PR's merge-base with `main`; temp-root-only trust gate)
- **candidate** = `2dcfc944` (PR head; content-bound outbound provenance)

The driver writes a generated report under the real OpenClaw temp root, stages it into `media/outbound` (which moves it outside the temp root), then performs the host-read reload exactly as the channel adapter does. Environment: macOS (Darwin 25.4.0, arm64), Node v26.0.0.

```text
# baseline (301213a05f) — same source sha256 50fcd4b2…
[baseline] staged path   = …/media/outbound/pr90579-proof-…---<uuid>.html
[baseline] staged under media/outbound = true
[baseline] staged outside temp root    = true
[baseline] RESULT: REJECTED — staged reload host-read BLOCKED
[baseline]         LocalMediaAccessError: hostReadCapability permits only validated plain-text documents and trusted generated HTML reports for local reads

# candidate (2dcfc944) — identical source sha256 50fcd4b2…
[candidate] staged path   = …/media/outbound/pr90579-proof-…---<uuid>.html
[candidate] staged under media/outbound = true
[candidate] staged outside temp root    = true
[candidate] RESULT: DELIVERED — staged reload host-read ALLOWED (144 bytes, sha==source? true)
[candidate] TAMPER CHECK: ✅ replaced bytes REJECTED — LocalMediaAccessError: hostReadCapability permits only validated plain-text documents and trusted generated HTML reports for local reads
```

This is the exact production silent-drop (`LocalMediaAccessError: hostReadCapability permits only validated plain-text documents and trusted generated HTML reports for local reads`) on baseline, and delivery on candidate — with the live tamper step confirming the content binding rejects swapped bytes at the same staged path. It is deterministic, runs in seconds through the real `loadWebMedia` gate, and needs no Telegram client, Mantis SUT, or host-read SUT config. This is the staged-reload-with-host-read path the earlier Mantis run did not exercise (its SUT did not propagate `hostReadCapability`, so both lanes bypassed the gate).

### Original production trace + unit-level confirmation

Behavior addressed: when an agent reply emits `MEDIA:/tmp/openclaw/<file>.html`, OpenClaw's outbound pipeline loads the file once (`/tmp/openclaw/...` is inside the tmp root, so trusted), stages a copy to `~/.openclaw/media/outbound/<file>---<uuid>.html`, and then the Telegram channel adapter calls `loadWebMedia(stagedPath)` a second time. Before this patch the second call rejected because the staged path is no longer under `/tmp/openclaw/`, and the Telegram message was silently dropped. After this patch the staged path is trusted **iff** its bytes match the recorded provenance digest, and delivery proceeds.

Real environment tested: live OpenClaw on macOS 26.4 (Darwin 25.4.0, arm64), Node v26.0.0, Telegram Bot API channel (polling). The original silent-drop was reproduced 4 times on the unpatched build (#90557). For this PR the patched code path was exercised in the repository test harness, since the CI checkout cannot drive a live Telegram bot end-to-end.

Evidence after fix (PR head):

```text
node scripts/generate-kysely-types.mjs --verify
# (exit 0 — generated Kysely types match the schema, incl. sha256 / size_bytes columns)

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/media/web-media.test.ts src/media/outbound-attachment.test.ts
 ✓ media  src/media/web-media.test.ts (79 tests)
 ✓ media  src/media/outbound-attachment.test.ts (5 tests)
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  84 passed (84)
```

Key new coverage:

```text
# Replacement attack (A1): bytes swapped after the marker → rejected
✓ rejects an outbound-staged HTML whose bytes were replaced after the provenance marker (content-bound trust)
# Provenance row records the real sha256 + size_bytes (no hardcoded digest)
✓ writes a trusted-generated-html provenance row keyed by the staged file's realpath
# Marker write failure (A2): propagated + staged file unlinked
✓ propagates a marker write failure and best-effort unlinks the staged file
```

What was not tested from this checkout: live Telegram Bot API delivery, because the CI environment lacks bot credentials. The original silent-drop behavior was verified in production (#90557, 4 reproductions with a `console.error` probe in the installed `dist`); the proof here demonstrates the trusted-host-read gate now accepts the same staged path **only when its bytes match the recorded digest** and rejects a tampered or unmarked staged file. The same instrumented probe can be re-run on a build containing this PR to confirm end-to-end delivery if needed.

target.title: test: migrate src/commands tests to shared temp dir helpers
target.body:
## Summary

- Migrate 4 test files in `src/commands/` from raw `fs.mkdtemp`/`fs.mkdtempSync` to shared temp dir helpers (`createSuiteTempRootTracker` / `withTempDir`)
- Files: `doctor-plugin-manifests.test.ts`, `migrate/apply.test.ts`, `doctor-security.test.ts`, `agents.add.test.ts`
- No user-visible behavior change

## Background

Part of the ongoing temp-dir migration. `createSuiteTempRootTracker()` is the standard helper for suite-scoped temp directories, providing automatic cleanup in `afterAll` and avoiding disk leaks from raw `mkdtemp` calls.

## What Problem This Solves

Relates to gap finding: `tempdir-batch03`

4 test files in `src/commands/` use raw `fs.mkdtemp`/`fs.mkdtempSync` instead of shared helpers, causing:
1. **Disk leaks** — temp dirs created but never cleaned up (P0: zero `rm` calls)
2. **Inconsistent patterns** — the project has standardized on shared helpers

## Why This Change Was Made

Migrates Batch 03 test files with no cleanup:

- `doctor-plugin-manifests.test.ts`: tracked array + `makeTrustedBundledPluginsDir` using `mkdtempSync`, never cleaned
- `migrate/apply.test.ts`: module-level `mkdtempSync` without any cleanup
- `doctor-security.test.ts`: `fs.mkdtemp` in `withExecApprovalsFile` helper, never cleaned
- `agents.add.test.ts`: `fs.mkdtemp` in `withAgentsAddStateRoot` helper, never cleaned

## User Impact

Test-only. Reduces `/tmp` pressure, cleaner CI.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: 内部重构，无用户可见行为变化
Real environment tested: 本地开发环境 (Linux x86_64, Node 24)
Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm test src/commands/doctor-plugin-manifests.test.ts src/commands/migrate/apply.test.ts src/commands/doctor-security.test.ts src/commands/agents.add.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix:

```console
$ pnpm test src/commands/doctor-plugin-manifests.test.ts src/commands/migrate/apply.test.ts src/commands/doctor-security.test.ts src/commands/agents.add.test.ts

 Test Files  4 passed (4)
      Tests  50 passed (50)
```

Observed result after fix: 50 tests pass across all 4 migrated test files, matching baseline behavior
What was not tested: full CI suite (triggered on push to remote)

target.title: feat(agents): add per-agent env subprocess contract
target.body:
Related: #93425

## Summary

- **Fix classification:** Root-cause fix for the public `agents.list[].env` config contract and ACP configured-owner authorization boundary.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** Ready for maintainer review after focused regression coverage, real subprocess smoke proof, public docs coverage, and local review of the full rescue diff.
- **Root cause:** `agents.list[].env` became a public agent config contract, but ACP paths could identify the runtime harness agent (`codex`) instead of the configured OpenClaw owner (`reviewer` / `codex-acp`) when resolving env, session ownership, and subagent allowlist authorization.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The fix keeps the ACP runtime agent as the harness id while resolving per-agent env, session identity, store ownership, workspace inheritance, spawned ACP session keys, and subagent policy authorization from the configured OpenClaw owner. That repairs the source-of-truth boundary instead of adding downstream special cases.
- **Why it matters / User impact:** Operators can configure one `agents.list[].env` entry and expect it to reach that agent's real spawned CLI, `exec`, ACP/ACPX, and stdio MCP subprocess boundaries, including configured ACP wrappers around a shared harness agent.
- **What did NOT change:** This does not introduce a metadata migration, change `SessionAcpMeta.agent` semantics, bypass host env sanitization, or expand stdio MCP server-local env precedence; server-local MCP env still overrides inherited per-agent env only for that MCP process.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** The canonical owner is the configured OpenClaw agent id from `agents.list[]` / ACP session keys; the ACP harness id remains only the runtime adapter target. Env lookup, session key ownership, resume/storage ownership, workspace inheritance, transcript persistence, and subagent `allowAgents` policy now use that same owner boundary. Public documentation describes precedence, blocked keys, default behavior, and literal-secret handling for the user-facing config surface.
- **Patch quality notes:** The patch is limited to ACP owner/env routing, ACP spawn owner persistence/policy, focused regression tests, and public contract docs. Existing type escapes are test-fixture casts for partial `OpenClawConfig` objects. Added default returns in ACPX/MCP paths preserve old no-env/no-feature behavior when a capability is absent; they do not create a new permissive fallback. It avoids fallback shims and preserves existing default behavior when `agents.list[].env` is omitted.

## Linked context

- Related issue: #93425 asks for per-agent environment injection into spawned subprocesses.
- This PR now covers the public `agents.list[].env` contract across CLI, `exec`, ACP/ACPX sessions, and stdio MCP subprocesses.
- Related open PR scan / canonical scope: this is a rescue of the existing open PR for #93425, not a new competing fix. The canonical contract surface remains `agents.list[].env` plus existing host env sanitization; no separate broader public-config PR is being introduced here.
- Out of scope: no new secret provider, no metadata migration, no ACP protocol change, no default env injection when the field is omitted, and no change to stdio MCP server-local env precedence.
- The previous review state called out ACP configured-agent ownership, public docs, security-boundary review, and maintainer review needs; those are mapped below under **Current review state**.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A configured ACP agent such as `reviewer` or `codex-acp` can wrap harness agent `codex`; its `agents.list[].env` must still be injected into the spawned ACP runtime while runtime initialization continues to target `codex`.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw repository with the PR rescue branch, a production `createExecTool` gateway execution that spawns a real child process, focused Vitest suites for ACP manager/spawn boundaries, schema help quality tests, docs formatting, and local diff review.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a real subprocess smoke through the production exec tool with `agents.list[].env` configured for agent `probe`, then ran focused validation commands: `corepack pnpm exec node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.initialize-session.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.runtime-handles.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`; `corepack pnpm exec node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`; `corepack pnpm exec node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.runtime-config.config.ts src/config/schema.hints.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`; `corepack pnpm format:docs:check`; `corepack pnpm docs:check-mdx`.
- **Evidence after fix:** The real subprocess smoke passed through production `createExecTool` / gateway exec and printed the child process env below. The configured allowed env value reached the child process, while blocked overrides did not leak: `OPENCLAW_AGENT_ENV` is present, `LD_PRELOAD` is absent, and the configured blocked `PATH` override is not present.

  ```text
  Warning: background execution is disabled; running synchronously.

  {"OPENCLAW_AGENT_ENV":"visible-to-real-child","LD_PRELOAD":null,"PATH":"[local path redacted]
  ```

  Focused ACP manager tests passed, including the regression where `sessionKey: agent:reviewer:acp:binding:discord:default:agent-env` initializes harness `agent: codex` with `env: { OPENCLAW_AGENT_ENV: "visible-to-owner" }`, and the restart/re-ensure regression where persisted `meta.agent: "codex"` still resolves owner env from the configured session key. ACP spawn tests passed, including the regression where `allowAgents: ["codex"]` no longer authorizes a subagent request for configured owner `codex-acp`, and the allowed-owner regression where `sessions_spawn({ runtime: "acp", agentId: "codex-acp" })` returns a child session key matching `agent:codex-acp:acp:*` while initializing runtime `agent: "codex"`. Schema/docs checks passed.
- **Observed result after fix:** Per-agent env reaches a real spawned `exec` subprocess and the ACP fresh initialization, persisted runtime re-ensure, and configured ACP `sessions_spawn` owner paths without changing the harness runtime id; blocked loader/PATH-style overrides stay filtered; subagent allowlists must explicitly allow the configured owner before that owner-scoped env can be used; public docs now describe precedence, blocked env keys, and literal secret guidance for the new config field.
- **What was not tested:** No external provider-backed live ACP service was invoked; ACP behavior is validated at the OpenClaw ACP manager/spawn subprocess boundary with regression tests, while the real child-process smoke covers the production exec subprocess env boundary requested by review.

## Tests and validation

- **Target test file:** `src/acp/control-plane/manager.initialize-session.test.ts`, `src/acp/control-plane/manager.runtime-handles.test.ts`, `src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts`, `src/config/schema.hints.test.ts`, and `src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** Configured ACP owners (`reviewer`, `codex-acp`) wrap harness agent `codex`; env lookup, session key ownership, restart re-ensure, spawn persistence, and subagent allowlist authorization must follow the configured owner while runtime initialization still targets `codex`.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** These tests exercise the exact boundary where env can be lost: ACP manager initialization, persisted runtime handle re-ensure, and ACP `sessions_spawn` session ownership. Broader provider-backed tests would be slower and less direct for this ownership bug.
- Focused validation covered ACP manager env injection, ACP spawn owner identity, config help strings, docs formatting, and MDX docs checks.
- ClawSweeper commit review was rerun after the ACP spawn owner fix and reported the final diff ready for local submit readiness.

## Risk checklist

- **Risk labels considered:** `merge-risk: compatibility`, `merge-risk: security-boundary`, `size: L`, public config/schema surface.
- **Risk explanation:** The highest-risk area is the public `agents.list[].env` operator contract because it affects child-process environment inheritance, ACP session identity, and subagent authorization for configured ACP aliases. The patch keeps env opt-in, preserves blocked loader/search-path/shell-hook/PATH/credential-control filtering, keeps literal-secret guidance in docs, and avoids changing existing sessions metadata semantics. The diff is larger than ideal because one public contract must be wired consistently across CLI, exec, ACP/ACPX, stdio MCP, tools-effective fingerprinting, schema/help, and docs; splitting only the ACP owner repair from the public docs would leave the PR in a misleading partially documented state.
- **Why acceptable:** The behavior is bounded to child-process spawn boundaries and configured ACP ownership. Existing configs without `agents.list[].env` keep their default behavior, stdio MCP server-local env precedence is documented rather than changed, and the regressions lock the configured-owner/harness-agent split plus the owner-based allowlist check that caused the review findings. ClawSweeper commit review passed on the final diff, and focused tests cover each changed execution boundary instead of relying only on broad compile/lint signals.

## Current review state

### Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- **RF-001 — maintainer-decision/advisory:** The PR still had `status: ⏳ waiting on author` when local work started. This update addresses the author-side findings below; the label can be reassessed by maintainers or bot review after the updated branch/body is submitted.
- **RF-002 — fixed:** ACP configured-agent env ownership is now covered across fresh initialization and runtime re-ensure. The manager resolves env from the configured owner encoded in the ACP session key while keeping runtime `agent` as the harness id.
- **RF-003 — fixed:** Public docs now describe `agents.list[].env` precedence, blocked key filtering, and literal-secret guidance.
- **RF-004 — fixed:** Configured ACP agents wrapping a harness id no longer silently drop owner env. Regression coverage verifies configured owner env reaches harness `codex` initialization and persisted re-ensure.
- **RF-005 — fixed:** The public config surface is documented in schema help and gateway agent config docs, including child-process scope, blocked keys, MCP server-local precedence, and secret-handling guidance.
- **RF-006 — fixed with maintainer-visible risk disclosure:** The security-sensitive subprocess env change remains opt-in and sanitized, with risk boundaries documented above. Maintainer acceptance of the operator contract is still a review decision, but the author-side proof and documentation gaps have been addressed.
- **RF-007 — maintainer-decision:** The change still deserves maintainer review because it touches public config, ACP ownership, and child-process security semantics. This body now surfaces the contract boundary and risk tradeoffs instead of hiding them behind green tests.
- **RF-008 — fixed:** ACP env is resolved from the configured owner rather than only the harness id in `manager.initialize-session`, with focused regression coverage for `agent:reviewer:acp:*` sessions initializing harness `codex`.
- **RF-009 — fixed:** `agents.list[].env` public help text now describes subprocess scope, precedence, blocked keys, and literal-secret guidance.

target.title: fix(tui): show busy startup loader during post-connect initialization [AI-assisted]
target.body:
> [AI-assisted] This PR was generated using Claude Code.

## Summary

- **Problem:** During Terminal Hatch onboarding (and any local/gateway TUI connect), `client.onConnected` in `src/tui/tui.ts` flipped the connection status to `"local ready"`/`"connected"` immediately and then awaited post-connect initialization (`refreshAgents()`, `restoreRememberedSession()`, `loadHistory()`) without ever setting a busy **activity** status. `renderStatus()` only renders the spinner/loader when `isTuiBusyActivityStatus(activityStatus)` is true, and `"idle"` is not in that set — so no loader rendered while init ran. The status line read "ready" while the UI was actually still starting up, making the TUI look frozen/laggy for ~2s after each prompt during hatch.
- **Why it matters now:** This is a first-run onboarding responsiveness regression (`bug`, `regression`, P2) reported against Terminal Hatch. A prior fix PR (#f02f570b3f69) was closed unmerged because it introduced a startup-error rendering regression and lacked real behavior proof.
- **Intended outcome:** The TUI shows the busy status loader ("starting up") for the duration of post-connect init, then clears to the normal ready line once init actually finishes.
- **Out of scope:** Any underlying init latency optimization (the async work itself is unchanged); reconnect UX beyond reusing the same busy-state path.
- **Success:** Loader renders during onConnected init and clears on completion; the final ready state is byte-for-byte unchanged; startup-failure still renders the `"startup failed"` text (the prior PR's regression is avoided).
- **Reviewer focus:** The `onConnected` restructure in `src/tui/tui.ts` — specifically that the busy activity status is cleared on **both** the success path and the `.catch` startup-failure path (so the `"startup failed"` static status still renders), and that the post-init ready status is unchanged.

## Linked context

Closes #74385

Related: prior closed fix attempt f02f570b3f69 (closed unmerged — startup-error regression + missing real behavior proof).

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner? No — triaged from the `clawsweeper:queueable-fix` / `clawsweeper:source-repro` shortlist.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: TUI looks frozen/laggy for ~2s during Terminal Hatch onboarding because client.onConnected flips the status to "local ready" and runs async post-connect init (agent refresh, session restore, history load) without setting a busy activity status, so renderStatus() never shows the loader.
- Real environment tested: Windows 10 LTSC, Node v22.17.1, OpenClaw source tree at upstream main 0b698709d8.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran `node --import tsx .tmp/startup-proof.mjs`, which imports the REAL exported helpers from `src/tui/tui.ts` (`isTuiBusyActivityStatus`, `isTuiActiveRunActivityStatus`, `resolveStartupActivityStatus`) and prints the actual startup-loader decision for the onConnected post-connect init window — covering both fresh connect and reconnect with an active run.
- Evidence after fix (terminal capture):
```
PROOF_START
# Fresh connect (no active run): TUI claims the starting-up loader
idle               busy=false activeRun=false -> startupClaim=starting up
connecting         busy=false activeRun=false -> startupClaim=starting up
starting local runtime busy=false activeRun=false -> startupClaim=starting up
# Reconnect with an active run: reconnect-owned streaming is PRESERVED
streaming          busy=true activeRun=true -> startupClaim=streaming
running            busy=true activeRun=true -> startupClaim=running
finishing context  busy=true activeRun=true -> startupClaim=finishing context
# Loader renders only for busy statuses (renderStatus gate)
renderStatus loaderOn('starting up')=true
renderStatus loaderOn('idle')=false
PROOF_END
```
- Observed result after fix: On a fresh connect the busy "starting up" loader renders during post-connect init, then clears to the static "local ready | idle" line once init finishes (ready state unchanged). On reconnect where `reconnectStreamingWatchdog()` has already restored `streaming` for a still-active run, `resolveStartupActivityStatus` now PRESERVES that status instead of overwriting it with `starting up`, and the post-init idle clear is gated so an in-flight run adopted by `loadHistory()` (which sets `streaming`) keeps streaming — reconnect no longer hides active work behind the startup loader.
- What was not tested: The interactive macOS Terminal Hatch onboarding flow was not reproduced locally — the TUI requires an interactive TTY (`Crestodian needs an interactive TTY`) and Windows cannot boot it under a PTY reliably enough to capture visible frames; the `tui-pty-harness.e2e.test.ts` behavioral coverage runs in the Linux PTY CI shard with a fake backend. No live gateway or embedded runtime was driven end-to-end on a physical machine, so a real interactive TUI/Hatch recording is still outstanding. Per the ClawSweeper review, a maintainer can capture that via: `@openclaw-mantis visual task: capture OpenClaw TUI during slow post-connect initialization and verify the starting up loader appears without hiding active streaming after reconnect.`
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):
```
BEFORE_START
# main: "starting up" is NOT a busy status, so renderStatus never shows the loader during init
isTuiBusyActivityStatus('starting up') = false
onConnected fresh connect      -> loaderOn=false <static text> "starting local runtime | starting up"  (looks frozen ~2s)
# main + prior PR head: onConnected UNCONDITIONALLY set "starting up" then UNCONDITIONALLY cleared to "idle"
onConnected reconnect w/ active run -> startupClaim overwrites reconnect-owned "streaming" with "starting up" (active run hidden)
post-init idle clear after loadHistory adoption -> overwrites adopted "streaming" with "idle" (in-flight run hidden)
BEFORE_END
```
```

## Tests and validation

Commands run locally (Windows, Node v22.17.1):

- `pnpm tsgo` — passed (TypeScript strict, no errors).
- `pnpm lint` — passed (oxlint, full shard run).
- `pnpm format:check` — the three changed files are clean (the 55 unrelated format failures pre-exist on main and are an environment artifact of Node 22.17 vs the required 22.19; not introduced here).
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/tui/tui.test.ts -t "isTuiBusyActivityStatus"` — the new unit assertion fails before the fix (`expected false to be true`) and passes after.
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts src/tui/tui-session-actions.test.ts` — 171 passed, no regressions.
- `pnpm build` — core compilation (tsdown) succeeded; the post-build `write-cli-startup-metadata` step fails only because this machine runs Node 22.17.1 (< required 22.19.0) — unrelated to this change; CI runs Node 22.19+.
- `pnpm check:host-env-policy:swift` — skipped on Windows (no Swift toolchain).

Regression coverage added:

- `src/tui/tui.test.ts` — unit assertion `isTuiBusyActivityStatus("starting up") === true` (deterministic, fails before / passes after).
- `src/tui/tui-pty-harness.e2e.test.ts` — PTY behavioral test that drives the real `runTui`/`onConnected` path with a slow post-connect init and asserts the busy `"starting up"` loader renders before `"local ready"` (runs in the Linux PTY CI shard; not reliably bootable under tsx+PTY on Windows).

What failed before this fix: `isTuiBusyActivityStatus("starting up")` returned `false`, so `renderStatus()` took the static-text branch and no loader rendered during onConnected init.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? **Yes** — intentionally. The TUI now shows a busy "starting up" loader during post-connect initialization instead of a falsely "ready" idle line. The final ready state is unchanged.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? **No.**

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? **No.** No CODEOWNERS secops paths touched.

What is the highest-risk area? The `client.onConnected` startup path, specifically that a thrown init error still renders the `"startup failed"` status text (the regression that sank the prior PR).

How is that risk mitigated? The success path clears `"starting up"` to `"idle"` only when no active run owns the status line — `loadHistory()` returning an adopted in-flight run, or a reconnect-owned `streaming`/`running`/`finishing context` status, is left intact so active work is not hidden. The `.catch` startup-failure path still clears to `"idle"` before `setConnectionStatus("startup failed", 5000)` so `renderStatus()` takes the static-text branch for failure/ready states. The "starting up" claim itself is gated by `resolveStartupActivityStatus` so it never overwrites reconnect-owned streaming. The post-init ready status string and TTL are unchanged.

## Current review state

Next action: CI (including the Linux PTY shard) and maintainer review.

Waiting on: Linux CI to run the new PTY behavioral regression (not bootable on the contributor's Windows machine); maintainer review of the `onConnected` busy-state clear-on-failure invariant.

Bot/reviewer comments addressed: none yet (fresh PR).
